challenges.prompt: How does the browser display this markup?
options: A large page title, a medium section heading, and a smaller sub-section heading, decreasing in size and prominence.
Three lines of identical large bold text, since all heading tags look the same.
The text appears italic because heading tags emphasize their content.
Nothing visible — heading tags only affect search engines, not what the browser shows.
challenges.explanation: Heading tags carry a hierarchy: <h1> is the top-level page title and renders largest, with <h2> and <h3> nesting beneath it at progressively smaller default sizes. The number signals how high-level the heading is to browsers, search engines, and screen readers alike.

challenges.prompt: This blog post is meant to have one main topic. What's wrong?
challenges.code:
All About Coffee

Coffee is a beloved morning ritual.

Brewing Methods

There are many ways to brew a cup.
options:
The

tags should come before the headings.
There are two <h1> tags — a page should have only one <h1>, so "Brewing Methods" should be an <h2>.

The paragraphs are missing closing

tags.
Heading text must be wrapped in <strong> to appear bold.
challenges.explanation: Each page should use exactly one <h1> to represent its single main topic, like the title of a book. "Brewing Methods" is a major section within the page, so it belongs in an <h2>. Multiple <h1> tags confuse search engines and screen readers.

challenges.prompt: Complete the tags so 'Warning:' is marked as important (bold) and 'loved' is marked as vocal stress (italic).
challenges.codeTemplate:
<{{blank}}>Warning: Never leave candles unattended.

She did not just like it — she <{{blank}}>loved it.
challenges.explanation: <strong> marks text as important and renders bold by default, so it wraps the safety word 'Warning:'. <em> marks emphasized text and renders italic by default, matching the vocal stress on 'loved'. Choose these tags for their meaning, not just their appearance.
